justification: Economic wellbeing perception is inherently multi-dimensional, spanning macro-level satisfaction (p1), personal satisfaction (p2), intergenerational retrospective comparison (p3), and prospective comparison (p4); no single item captures the full construct. All four items use compatible ordinal scales (5–6 categories) anchored around satisfaction or comparative standing, making mean aggregation appropriate. Mean scoring preserves the ordinal spacing and the resulting composite can be binned into ≤5 ordered categories for the bridge model, reducing item-level measurement error while maximizing construct coverage.

Job quality is multi-dimensional but the seven available items mix binary, nominal-categorical, and ordinal scales, making direct summation inappropriate across all items. The three selected items—social security coverage (p43), relative income self-assessment (p45_1), and satisfaction with advancement opportunities (p46_1)—together capture the core quality dimensions (benefits, compensation, career prospects) and share an ordinal or quasi-ordinal structure with the highest entropies in the cluster (H=1.54, 1.62, 1.81). PCA on the first component extracts the dominant latent quality dimension while handling residual scale differences, and the resulting scores are binned into 5 quantile categories for the bridge model.

Employment precarity is best captured by combining perceived job-loss risk (p48, H=1.86), desire to change jobs (p49, H=1.30), and recent active job-search behavior (p56, H=1.42), which together span the attitudinal and behavioral dimensions of precarity. The remaining items either have very low entropy (p50 H=0.41, p58 H=0.67, p59_x H≈0.58–0.61) indicating limited discriminatory power, or are numeric with sparse distributions (p57). PCA on the first component accommodates the mixed ordinal/binary structure of the three selected items and extracts the dominant precarity signal, binned into 5 quantile categories for the bridge.

p23 (perceived necessity of labor unions, H=2.21) shares the highest entropy with p24 but has stronger face validity as a direct attitudinal measure of union necessity, which is the conceptual core of this construct. p24 is described as open-ended/ordinal and likely captures opinions about union characteristics rather than a clean attitudinal stance, making it less suitable as a single bridge variable. p22 (worker rights respect) is conceptually adjacent but taps a distinct perception; given the construct's moderate importance (3/5) and the high entropy of p23 alone, a single well-defined ordinal item is sufficient for the bridge without risking scale-incompatibility artifacts from aggregation.

Human capital is inherently multi-dimensional, requiring coverage of both objective attainment (p67, highest education completed, H=2.31) and job-specific adequacy perceptions (p65, appropriate education for job, H=2.79; p66, necessity of specialized training, H=2.25). These three items carry the highest entropy in the cluster and together capture the supply-side human capital stock alongside its perceived match to labor market demands. PCA on the first component handles the differing category counts (14, 7, 11) and extracts the dominant human capital dimension, with scores binned into 5 quantile categories; p64 (education at first job) and p71–p72 (training participation/utility, H≈0.55–0.56) are excluded due to temporal redundancy with p67 and near-floor entropy respectively.
